title: Cat Swollen Toes and Infected Nail Bed: Care & Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about feline nail bed infections (paronychia), when to see a vet, and why photos are vital for triaging your cat's swollen toe.
content_html:
What Is an Infected Nail Bed or Swollen Toe?

An infection of the tissue surrounding the claw is medically referred to as paronychia. When a cat has a swollen toe or infected nail bed, it typically presents as redness, warmth, and sensitivity. You may notice your cat limping, over-grooming the area, or see a discharge of pus or crusting around the base of the nail. Common causes include traumatic nail breaks, ingrown claws, bacterial infections, or fungal issues like ringworm.

Is It an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. A swollen toe is rarely a life-threatening emergency, but it is a painful condition that requires prompt medical attention. If the infection is left untreated, it can spread to the bone (osteomyelitis) or lead to systemic illness. You should aim to have your cat seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. However, if your cat is also lethargic, hiding, or has a fever, contact an urgent care facility sooner.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the affected paw is one of the most helpful things you can do for a triage professional. High-quality images allow a vet to assess the degree of inflammation, look for signs of a 'foxtail' or foreign body, and determine if the nail itself is fractured. This visual information helps prioritize your cat’s case and provides a baseline to monitor if the swelling improves or worsens before your appointment.

faqs.question: Can I use human antibiotic ointment on my cat's toe?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid using human ointments like Neosporin, as they often contain ingredients that are toxic to cats if ingested during grooming. Always consult your vet before applying topical treatments.

faqs.question: How can I help my cat stay comfortable until the vet visit?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat indoors and in a clean environment to prevent further contamination. If your cat allows it, a brief soak in lukewarm water with a very small amount of diluted Betadine (to the color of weak tea) may help, but stop immediately if it causes stress or pain.

faqs.question: What might the vet do to treat the infection?
faqs.answer: The veterinarian will likely perform a physical exam and may take a small sample (cytology) to identify the bacteria or fungus. Treatment typically includes oral antibiotics, pain relief, and sometimes a protective bandage or an Elizabethan collar to prevent licking.
